summary: Gruber awards Paul Thurrott his 'Jackass of the Week' honor for a hypocritical price comparison between Mac Pro and Dell Precision workstations. Thurrott initially configured the Dell with a cheaper processor, and even after correcting it, configured the Dell as a 32-bit system to keep its price lower. His justification that 64-bit 'isn't mainstream on Windows' directly contradicts his own writing from the previous week, where he praised Windows XP x64 Edition and criticized Apple for being two years behind on 64-bit support. Gruber highlights this blatant double standard as the defining reason for the award.
key_points: Thurrott's original Mac Pro vs. Dell comparison used mismatched processors, favoring Dell, and had to be corrected
Even after correction, Thurrott configured the Dell as a cheaper 32-bit system while the Mac Pro was 64-bit capable
Thurrott justified omitting 64-bit on the Dell by claiming it wasn't mainstream on Windows
Just one week earlier, Thurrott had touted Windows XP x64 Edition as proof Apple was two years behind Microsoft on 64-bit
The contradiction reveals a consistent bias: any framing that makes Apple look worse gets applied regardless of consistency
money_quotes: His original post, now corrected, showed the Dell coming out cheaper, but he inexplicably configured the Dell with a cheaper processor in that comparison.
In the updated post, with both systems configured with 3.0 GHz Xeon CPUs, the Mac Pro comes out $500 cheaper.
But even still, he has configured the Dell as a (cheaper) 32-bit system.
So when the feature was announced at WWDC, it was a catch-up feature that Thurrott claims put Apple two years behind Microsoft.
But when it would have made Dell look even worse in a side-by-side pricing comparison, it's 'not exactly a mainstream configuration'.
key_insight: Thurrott's treatment of 64-bit computing as either a must-have advantage or an irrelevant detail depending on which framing makes Apple look worse reveals the intellectual dishonesty underlying his commentary.
